_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-13 Bleeding Control (BCON) (Revised 03/03/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-13: Bleeding Control (BCON) — MDHHS Approved 3/3/25, Reviewed 2025

sections.title: Bleeding Control — General
points: Indicated for significant traumatic or non-traumatic external hemorrhage
Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol and Soft Tissue/Orthopedic Injuries-Treatment Protocol
Apply direct pressure to the wound with clean gauze or dressing material
If bleeding is controlled, do not remove the dressing; reinforce as needed and reassess frequently

sections.title: Extremity Hemorrhage
points: If extremity bleeding is not controlled with direct pressure, apply a tourniquet
If the first tourniquet is unsuccessful, apply a second tourniquet adjacent to the first
Treat tourniquet-related pain per Pain Management protocol when appropriate
Document the location and time of tourniquet application and reassess bleeding control

sections.title: Junctional and Non-Compressible Hemorrhage
points: Junctional hemorrhage includes neck, axilla, shoulder, and groin wounds where a standard extremity tourniquet cannot be applied
Pack the wound with an MCA-approved hemostatic dressing if available; otherwise use clean gauze
Use as much dressing or gauze as needed and follow manufacturer directions for hemostatic products
Apply firm pressure until bleeding stops, approximately 3–5 minutes, then leave the dressing in place and wrap to maintain pressure
Use caution when packing neck wounds: avoid airway compromise and do not encircle the neck
Packing penetrating wounds of the head, chest, or abdomen is contraindicated

sections.title: Wound Dressing and Monitoring
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-13: BCON — Dressing and Monitoring
points: After hemorrhage control is achieved: apply clean dry dressings; do NOT remove bandages or gauze — reinforce with additional dressings
Continuously reassess bleeding control during transport
If a hemostatic dressing is used, contact Medical Control to advise application, document the time, and send the packaging to the hospital
Monitor for signs of hemorrhagic shock — refer to Hemorrhagic Shock-Treatment Protocol if signs present
Transport per Trauma Triage and MCA Transport protocols

mca_selections_required.description: MCA must approve specific hemostatic dressing agents for use in wound packing (e.g., Combat Gauze, Celox, ChitoGauze, or other commercial hemostatic agents)

deterioration_flags: Active uncontrolled extremity hemorrhage after direct pressure — apply tourniquet; if unsuccessful, apply a second adjacent tourniquet
Junctional hemorrhage not controlled with direct pressure — pack with MCA-approved hemostatic dressing if available or clean gauze; firm pressure for approximately 3–5 minutes
Signs of hemorrhagic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status, pallor) — refer to Hemorrhagic Shock-Treatment Protocol; expedite transport
Hemostatic dressing used — contact Medical Control, document time, and send packaging with patient
scope_notes: Direct pressure, tourniquet application, pressure dressings, and wound packing: all levels
Wound packing with hemostatic dressing (junctional hemorrhage): all levels — per MCA-approved hemostatic agent
Hemostatic dressing products: per MCA-approved product and training
Hemorrhagic shock management: refer to Hemorrhagic Shock-Treatment Protocol for scope by level
